audit_status.source_base: Greek 1 Corinthians text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for servants/stewards, judgment, not beyond what is written, apostolic humility, fatherly admonition, kingdom in power.

verses.restored: Let a human calculate us this way: as attendants of Messiah and stewards of mysteries of the Divine.
Here, furthermore, it is sought among stewards that one be found trustworthy.
But to me it is a very small thing that I should be examined by you or by a human day; indeed I do not even examine myself.
For I am conscious of nothing against myself, but I have not been made righteous/just by this; the one examining me is the Lord.

Therefore judge nothing before the season, until the Lord comes, who will both bring to light the hidden things of darkness and make manifest the counsels of hearts; and then praise will come to each from the Divine.
These things, siblings, I transferred to myself and Apollos because of you, so that in us you may learn not beyond what has been written, so that no one is puffed up for one against another.
For who distinguishes you? And what do you have that you did not receive? But if you received it, why boast as though not receiving?
Already you are filled; already you became rich; apart from us you reigned. And would that you did reign, so that we also might reign with you.
For I think the Divine displayed us, the apostles, last, as condemned to death, because we became a spectacle to the world, both to messengers and to humans.
We are foolish because of Messiah, but you are wise in Messiah; we are weak, but you are strong; you are honored, but we dishonored.
Until the present hour we both hunger and thirst, are poorly clothed, are struck, and wander homeless,
and labor, working with our own hands. Being insulted, we bless; being persecuted, we endure;
being slandered, we encourage/appeal. We became as refuse of the world, scum of all things, until now.
I do not write these things shaming you, but admonishing you as my beloved children.
For though you may have ten thousand guardians in Messiah, yet not many fathers; for in Messiah Jesus through the good news I fathered you.
Therefore I urge you: become imitators of me.
Because of this I sent to you Timothy, who is my beloved and trustworthy child in the Lord, who will remind you of my ways in Messiah, just as I teach everywhere in every assembly.
Some were puffed up as though I were not coming to you.
But I will come to you quickly, if the Lord wills, and I will know not the word of those puffed up but the power.
For the reign of the Divine is not in word but in power.
What do you will? Shall I come to you with a rod, or in love and a spirit of gentleness?

translation_notes: Paul's father-language is pastoral and rhetorical; it should not be inflated into domination.
The kingdom/reign in power contrasts performative speech and puffed-up status with Spirit-backed reality.
